title: Plant tissues and organs — how plants are built
examTip: AQA loves asking you to name tissues in a leaf and say what each one does. Know all five tissue types by name, location and function — and be ready to compare xylem and phloem.
introTips: This lesson is about how plants are built from the inside out — right down to the tissues that do the actual work.
The leaf is AQA's favourite structure to ask about: you need to know all its tissues, where they sit, and what each one does.
Xylem and phloem come up constantly — in plant tissue questions AND in transpiration. Get them nailed here and the next lesson gets easier.
intro: You already know plants photosynthesise. But how does the leaf actually manage that? How does water get from the soil to a leaf ten metres up a tree? The answer is in the tissues — the specialised building blocks inside every plant organ. Once you see the structure, the function makes perfect sense.

keyPoints.heading: Plant organs — leaf, stem and root
keyPoints.content: Plants, like animals, have organs. The three main plant organs are the leaf, the stem and the root. Each one is made of several different tissues working together. The leaf is the main organ for photosynthesis. The stem supports the plant and carries materials between root and leaf. The root anchors the plant and absorbs water and minerals from the soil.
writeBullets: Three main plant organs: leaf, stem, root
Each organ contains several different tissues
Leaf = main site of photosynthesis
Stem = support + transport
Root = anchorage + water and mineral absorption

keyTerms.def: A structure made of several different tissues working together to carry out a specific function
A group of similar cells performing the same function

keyPoints.memTip: Three organs, three jobs: Leaf = light (photosynthesis), Stem = support (and transport), Root = resources (water + minerals). L, S, R — three letters, three functions.

keyPoints.heading: The tissues inside a leaf
keyPoints.content: A leaf is made of several distinct tissues, each with a specific role. The upper epidermis is a single thin, transparent layer — it lets light through without absorbing it. The palisade mesophyll sits just below: cells packed tightly with chloroplasts, this is where most photosynthesis happens. Below that is the spongy mesophyll, with irregular cells and large air spaces that allow gases to diffuse. The lower epidermis contains stomata (pores) controlled by guard cells — these regulate gas exchange and water loss. Running through the leaf are vascular bundles containing xylem and phloem, which supply water and remove sugars.
writeBullets: Upper epidermis: thin, transparent, lets light through
Palisade mesophyll: packed chloroplasts, site of most photosynthesis
Spongy mesophyll: large air spaces, allows gas diffusion
Lower epidermis: stomata + guard cells for gas exchange
Vascular bundles: contain xylem and phloem

keyTerms.def: Tightly packed cells below the upper epidermis, rich in chloroplasts; main site of photosynthesis
Loosely packed cells with air spaces that allow gas diffusion across the leaf
Pores in the lower epidermis that allow gas exchange; opened and closed by guard cells
Pairs of cells that surround each stoma and control its opening and closing
A strand of xylem and phloem tissue running through plant organs

keyPoints.memTip: From top to bottom in a leaf cross-section: Upper epidermis → Palisade → Spongy → Lower epidermis. Use UP → PS (Photosynthesis) → S (Spaces) → LE. Draw it once and label it — the visual sticks far better than reading a list.

keyPoints.heading: Xylem — carrying water upwards
keyPoints.content: You need water to reach every leaf. Xylem is the tissue that does this job. It carries water and dissolved minerals upwards from the roots to the rest of the plant. Xylem cells are dead — they have no cytoplasm, so they form hollow continuous tubes. Their walls are lignified (strengthened with lignin), which makes them waterproof and gives them structural strength. Because the cells are dead and hollow, water can flow through without obstruction.
writeBullets: Xylem carries water and dissolved minerals upwards from roots
Cells are dead — no cytoplasm, hollow tubes
Walls are lignified (contain lignin) — waterproof and strong
Continuous hollow tubes allow uninterrupted water flow
One-way transport: always upwards

keyTerms.def: Plant tissue made of dead, hollow, lignified cells that transport water and minerals upwards from roots
A tough, waterproof substance deposited in the cell walls of xylem vessels, providing structural strength

keyPoints.memTip: Xylem = X marks the spot — water goes UP (X for eXit, water eXits the roots). Dead cells = hollow tubes = like pipes in a wall. Lignified = like reinforced concrete. If you remember 'dead, hollow, lignified', you've got the three key xylem facts.

keyPoints.heading: Phloem — carrying sugars to where they're needed
keyPoints.content: Once the leaf makes sugars by photosynthesis, those sugars need to reach every part of the plant — roots, growing tips, storage organs. Phloem does this. It transports dissolved sugars (mainly sucrose) from leaves to all other parts of the plant, in a process called translocation. Unlike xylem, phloem cells are living. They are made of sieve tube cells (with holes — sieve plates — at each end to allow flow) and companion cells (which support the sieve tubes with energy and metabolic activity). Phloem can move substances up or down depending on where they're needed.
writeBullets: Phloem transports dissolved sugars (mainly sucrose) from leaves to the rest of the plant
This process is called translocation
Phloem cells are living (unlike xylem)
Made of sieve tube cells + companion cells
Can transport up OR down — wherever sugars are needed

keyTerms.def: Living plant tissue that transports dissolved sugars (mainly sucrose) from leaves to the rest of the plant
The movement of dissolved sugars through phloem from where they are made to where they are used or stored
Living phloem cells with perforated end walls (sieve plates) that allow dissolved substances to flow through
Cells alongside sieve tubes that provide energy and support for phloem transport

keyPoints.memTip: Phloem = Food on the move. Translocation = the word for how plants move food. Sieve tube = picture a kitchen sieve — holes at each end let things flow through. Living cells need energy to load sugars into phloem — that's what companion cells are for.

tables.title: Xylem vs Phloem — key comparison
rows: Sieve tubes with perforated end walls

2.question: Which tissue in a leaf is most responsible for photosynthesis?
2.correctFeedback: Exactly — palisade mesophyll cells are packed with chloroplasts and sit right where light hits first. That's where most photosynthesis happens.
2.wrongFeedback: Palisade mesophyll is the answer. It's the layer just below the upper epidermis, filled with chloroplasts. The spongy layer is for diffusion; the epidermis layers protect and regulate — they don't do much photosynthesis.

3.question: Which of the following is true of xylem but NOT phloem?
options: It is found in vascular bundles
It transports substances through the plant
Its cells are dead and lignified
3.correctFeedback: Right — dead, lignified cells are the defining feature of xylem. Phloem cells are living.
3.wrongFeedback: Both xylem and phloem sit in vascular bundles and both transport substances. Only xylem carries water (not sucrose — that's phloem). The key difference is that xylem cells are dead and lignified; phloem cells are living.

commonMistakes: Saying xylem carries sugars and phloem carries water — it's the other way round. Xylem = water and minerals; phloem = sugars (sucrose).
Describing xylem cells as living — they are dead, hollow and lignified. That's what makes them good tubes.
Forgetting that phloem can move both up and down — xylem is one-way (upwards); phloem goes wherever the plant needs sugars.
Confusing 'translocation' (phloem, sugars) with 'transpiration' (water loss through leaves) — these are two different processes with similar-sounding names.
Leaving out companion cells when describing phloem — AQA mark schemes often want both sieve tube cells AND companion cells.
examTips: If a question asks you to compare xylem and phloem, use five features: substance transported, direction, alive/dead, structure, and wall lignification. Cover all five and you'll pick up every mark.
When describing the tissues of a leaf, go from top to bottom: upper epidermis → palisade mesophyll → spongy mesophyll → lower epidermis. AQA questions often follow this order.
The word 'translocation' is worth one mark every time you use it correctly in context. If you're talking about phloem moving sugars, say 'translocation' — don't just say 'transport'.

videoLinks.label: Leaf Structure and Tissues

revisionCardBullets: Three plant organs: leaf (photosynthesis), stem (support + transport), root (anchorage + absorption)
Leaf tissues top to bottom: upper epidermis → palisade mesophyll → spongy mesophyll → lower epidermis
Palisade mesophyll: packed chloroplasts, most photosynthesis happens here
Spongy mesophyll: large air spaces for gas diffusion
Stomata in lower epidermis; guard cells open and close them
Xylem: carries water and minerals UPWARDS; dead, hollow, lignified cells
Phloem: carries dissolved sugars (sucrose) up or down; living cells
Translocation = movement of sugars through phloem
Phloem = sieve tube cells + companion cells
Key difference: xylem cells are dead and lignified; phloem cells are living
